outlook.headline: Capacity tightness is broadening into a sector-wide tailwind
outlook.summary: Mentions accelerated to 10.5x, and the read-through is clearly constructive: nearly every company cites tight supply, high utilization, or customers pulling forward capacity additions. The tightest pockets are in AI/leading-edge, DRAM, and packaging/test, which should keep pricing, lead times, and supplier leverage favorable over the next 1-2 quarters. Watch for any evidence that new capacity comes online faster than demand or that bottlenecks simply shift downstream.

drivers.label: AI-led demand is outrunning available capacity
drivers.detail: Frontier-model and high-performance compute demand is forcing customers to add supply, with sold-out capacity and long lead times supporting the entire chain.

drivers.label: Customers are still adding fab and equipment capacity aggressively
drivers.detail: Foundry, DRAM, and leading-edge customers are expanding output, which keeps current supply tight and supports utilization for vendors into 2026Q1.

drivers.label: Downstream packaging and test remain constrained
drivers.detail: Packaging, assembly, and test capacity is still tight, extending the supply squeeze beyond wafer fabs and supporting suppliers tied to backend throughput.

drivers.label: Tight supply is enabling customer prebuys and queue priority
drivers.detail: Several reads point to customers paying up, prepaying, or using distributors to secure supply, which reinforces near-term scarcity.

drivers.label: A small set of logistics constraints can still cap realized supply
drivers.detail: Transport/liquefaction or other non-fab bottlenecks can delay shipment conversion even where demand is strong.

risks: Capacity additions at DRAM, foundry, or packaging nodes ramp faster than expected, easing shortages.
AI demand normalizes and queue pressure at suppliers like NVIDIA, Broadcom, and AMD starts to fade.
Non-fab bottlenecks, such as logistics or liquefaction constraints, limit how much of the demand converts into shipments.
